perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall exercise the same degree of care and protection with respect to the Confidential Information of the disclosing party that it exercises with respect to the protection of its own confidential informat ion of a similar nature but not less than a reasonable degree of care and shall prevent disclosure of the Confidential Information to anyone except to those of its employees that have a need to know such information solely in c onnection with the permitted use as set forth in Section 2 and who shall be required to observe the restrictions set forth in this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall exercise a degree of care and protection with respect to the Confidential Information of the disclosing party that it deems adequate, and shall endeavor to prevent disclosure of the Confidential Information to anyone except to those of its employees that may have a need to know such information in c onnection with the permitted use as set forth in Section 2 and who are generally expected to observe the restrictions set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the same degree of care...but not less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'a degree of care...that it deems adequate' introduces subjectivity. Replacing 'shall prevent disclosure' with 'shall endeavor to prevent disclosure' weakens the obligation. Using 'may have a need to know' instead of 'have a need to know' broadens the exception. Substituting 'shall be required to observe the restrictions' with 'are generally expected to observe the restrictions' makes adherence to the confidentiality agreement non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The South Carolina Trade Secrets Act requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy' of trade secrets. The modified text's subjective 'deems adequate' and weakened 'endeavor to prevent disclosure' do not guarantee reasonable efforts, potentially failing to meet the standard required by the Act, especially considering the high standard of care usually applied to trade secrets.

Short title. This chapter may be cited as the "South Carolina Trade Secrets Act". HISTORY: 1992 Act No. 437, Section 3, eff June 15, 1992; 1997 Act No. 38, Section 1, eff May 21, 1997. Effect of Amendment The 1997 amendment rewrote this section. SECTION 39-8-20. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, duties imposed by the common law, statute, contract, license, protective order, or other court or administrative order, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired by mistake or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Owner" means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to the trade secret is reposed. (4) "Person" means an individual, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (5) "Trade secret" means: (a) information including, but not limited to, a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, product, system, or process, design, prototype, procedure, or code that: (i) derives independent economic value, actual or

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated a 'reasonable degree of care' in protecting confidential information. The changed text replaces this objective standard with a subjective one ('deems adequate'), and weakens the obligation to prevent disclosure. This contradicts the spirit of the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act, which, while not explicitly mentioning 'reasonable degree of care,' focuses on preventing 'misappropriation' of trade secrets. S.C. Code § 39-8-20 defines 'misappropriation' to include unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. By weakening the obligation to actively prevent disclosure and allowing the receiving party to determine what care is 'adequate,' the modified contract increases the risk of misappropriation, thereby creating a contradiction with the general intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges that all such Confidential Information, and any improvements or deri vations of any intellectual property used in the course of such relationship, is and shall continue to be the ex clusive proprietary property of Champion, whether or not prepared in whole or in part by Company and whether or not disclosed to or entrusted to the custody of Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges that Confidential Information, and potential improvements of intellectual property used, may be considered the property of Champion. The determination of ownership will be made on a case-by-case basis, taking into account factors such as contribution and prior agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is and shall continue to be the exclusive proprietary property' to 'may be considered the property' introduces uncertainty. Adding 'The determination of ownership will be made on a case-by-case basis' creates ambiguity about ownership rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 261 states that patents have the attributes of personal property and parties involved may grant and convey title to the patent and inventions. Introducing a 'case-by-case basis' undermines the clear assignment of ownership inherent to the Patent Act, making it less clear who owns what, and opens the door to future disputes, when the law provides clear rules for assigning ownership.

Ownership; assignment Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writin
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly assigns ownership of confidential information and intellectual property improvements to Champion. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating that ownership 'may be considered' Champion's and will be determined 'on a case-by-case basis.' This contradicts 35 U.S.C. § 261, which, as stated in the Law Explanation, implies a clear assignment of ownership. The scraped snippet of 35 USC 261 states, 'patents shall have the attributes of personal property... Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing.' The modification undermines the ability to clearly assign ownership of intellectual property, making it less clear and opening it up to disputes. This creates a contradiction because it deviates from the principle of clear assignment as supported by the law snippet.

perturbation.original_text: Company will not at any time, directly or indirectly, manufactu re, sell, distribute, repair or overhaul any products or components thereof using any Confidential Information and durin g the term of this Agreement and for a period of 30 years thereafter, will not, directly or indirectly, manufact ure, sell, distribute, repair or overhaul any products or components the same or identical to the products or components manufactured by Company for or on behalf of Champion (or any other product or components thereof that is s old in competition to any such products or components manufactured by Company for or on behalf of Champion ) to or for any third party without the prior written approval of Champion.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will use commercially reasonable efforts not to manufacture, sell, distribute, repair or overhaul any products or components thereof using Confidential Information. For a reasonable period of time after this agreement, Company will endeavor not to manufacture, sell, distribute, repair, or overhaul any substantially similar products or components.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not at any time' with 'will use commercially reasonable efforts not to' and 'will not' with 'will endeavor not to' significantly weakens the prohibition on competition. Changing '30 years thereafter' to 'a reasonable period of time after this agreement' is highly ambiguous and could be interpreted in various ways. Replacing 'the same or identical' with 'substantially similar' invites disputes about what constitutes 'substantially similar'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Carolina Restraint of Trade Laws
perturbation.law_explanation: South Carolina law requires restraints of trade to be reasonable with respect to time and geographic scope. The modification's ambiguous 'reasonable period of time' offers no specific time frame. As such, the modified text weakens the clause and risks being deemed unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, which conflicts with what would normally be considered a 'reasonable' restraint in light of the Act.

Short title; definitions. This article may be cited as the "South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act." As used in this article, (a) "Person" shall include natural persons, corporations, trusts, partnerships, incorporated or unincorporated associations and any other legal entity. (b) "Trade" and "commerce" shall include the advertising, offering for sale, sale or distribution of any services and any property, tangible or intangible, real, personal or mixed, and any other article, commodity or thing of value wherever situate, and shall include any trade or commerce directly or indirectly affecting the people of this State. (c) "Documentary material" shall include the original or a copy of any book, record, report, memorandum, paper, communication, tabulation, map, chart, photograph, mechanical transcription or other tangible document or recording, wherever situate. (d) "Examination" of documentary material shall include the inspection, study or copying of any such material, and the taking of testimony under oath or acknowledgment in respect of any such documentary material or copy thereof. HISTORY: 1962 Code Section 66-71; 1971 (57) 369. SECTION 39-5-20. Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices unlawful; application of federal act. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to Section 5(a) (1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. HISTORY: 1962 Code Section 66-71.1; 1971 (57) 369. SECTION 39-5-35. Requiring certain insurance coverage as unfair trade practice. It shall be an unlawful trade practice under Section 39-5-20 for any person engaged in the business of lending money to make it a condition of obtaining a loan for the purchase of an automobile that the borrower carry full coverage comprehensive or fifty dollars collision coverage. HISTORY: 1979 Act No. 121 Section 5. SECTION 39-5-36. Resale of tickets for more than original price. (A) A person or firm who knowingly purchases a quantity of tickets for admission to an event which exceeds the maximum quantity posted by or on behalf of the original ticket seller at the point of original sale or printed on the tickets and intends to resell the tickets in excess of one dollar above the price charged by the original ticket seller violates the South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act and is subject to its provisions, penalties, and damages. (B) A person or firm who violates the provisions of Section 16-17-710(A) is subject to the provisions, penalties, and damages of the South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act. (C) A person or firm is not liable pursuant to this section with respect to tickets for which the person or firm is the original ticket seller. (D) For purposes of this section, the term "original ticket seller" means the issuer of the tickets or a person or firm who provides tick

perturbation.justification: The original contract contains specific restrictions on the company's ability to compete, including a defined period of 30 years. The modified contract introduces ambiguities by using terms like 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'reasonable period of time,' which weaken the original prohibitions. The law explanation states that South Carolina law requires restraints of trade to be reasonable with respect to time. By replacing a fixed term with 'reasonable period of time,' the modified contract creates an ambiguity that could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, thus contradicting the law. Snippet 2, referencing South Carolina Code § 39-5-20, declares unlawful 'unfair methods of competition'. While the snippet does not explicitly define 'reasonable,' the ambiguity introduced weakens the enforceability of the non-compete clause, making it potentially an unfair method of competition by not providing a clear and reasonable restriction as interpreted under the FTC Act, as stated in section (b).

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees and covenants tha t it will not release, show, ma ke available, disclose or deliver in any form or fashion all or any part of this information to any person who i s not a U.S. Citizen, a lawful permanent resident of the United States (i.e., valid “Gree n Card” holder – a Visa is irre levant for this purpose), or who has received Political Asylum in the United States without first having determined whe ther it is lawful to do so under U.S. laws including the EAR, ITAR , and O FAC. Further, Compan y will not release, s how, make available, disclose or deliver in any form or fashion all or any part of this information to any non- U.S. Person without having first obtained the necessary license or approval that may be re quired from the U.S. Departme nt of Commerce, Departmen t of State, Department of Treasury, or other agency, department, or of fice.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will, to the best of its knowledge, attempt to ensure that it does not release information to any person who is potentially not authorized under U.S. laws. Company will seek guidance from appropriate departments as it deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'to the best of its knowledge, attempt to ensure' is vague. The original language created a strong obligation, where this is more about trying. Also, 'potentially not authorized' is ambiguous and does not provide a clear standard. 'As it deems necessary' means that the company has ultimate discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: EAR regulations impose strict liability on companies that export controlled technology without a license. The original text adheres to this standard by placing a firm obligation on the company to not release information. By contrast, the edited text weakens this obligation by replacing it with a 'to the best of its knowledge' and 'attempt to ensure', thereby contradicting the strict liability standard imposed by the EAR.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a firm obligation to not release information to unauthorized individuals with a weaker obligation to 'attempt to ensure' it does not happen. This directly contradicts the strict liability standard often associated with export control regulations under the EAR, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text's clear prohibition is replaced with a 'best of its knowledge' standard, which provides a loophole and undermines the intended compliance with EAR regulations. The snippet from 15 CFR 734.2 indicates that the EAR applies, and the Law Explanation suggests that the EAR imposes strict liability. The change from a firm obligation to a 'best of its knowledge' attempt significantly weakens the legal requirement and creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that any suit, action, or procee ding seeking to enforce any provision of, or based on any matter arising out of or in c onnection with t his Agreement be brought in the State or Federal Courts situated in Greenville County, SC, that the parties hereby consent to the j urisdiction of any such court, and that the parties irrevocably waive any objection to the laying of venue of any s uch suit, action or proceeding in any such court or that any such court is an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that any suit, action, or proceeding seeking to enforce any provision of, or based on any matter arising out of or in connection with this Agreement may be brought in the State or Federal Courts situated in Greenville County, SC, and the parties consent to the jurisdiction of such court. The parties agree that any objection to the laying of venue of any such suit, action or proceeding in such court or that such court is an inconvenient forum will be reviewed on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'be brought' with 'may be brought' makes the forum selection clause permissive rather than mandatory. Removing 'irrevocably waive' and replacing it with 'will be reviewed on a case-by-case basis' allows a party to challenge venue later.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district where it might have been brought. Enforceable forum selection clauses typically prevent such transfers. By changing the language to make the forum selection non-mandatory and allowing venue challenges on a case-by-case basis, the agreement now arguably contradicts the spirit of enforcing forum selection clauses, making it more likely that a suit could be transferred despite the agreement's intent.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract and the spirit of forum selection clauses, potentially undermining their enforceability under 28 U.S.C. § 1404. By changing 'be brought' to 'may be brought,' the agreement introduces ambiguity regarding mandatory venue. More importantly, replacing 'irrevocably waive' with a case-by-case review of objections significantly weakens the clause. While 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) (as shown in both snippets) permits transfer for convenience, enforceable forum selection clauses are intended to prevent such transfers, establishing a pre-agreed venue. The revised language makes the venue subject to judicial discretion, going against the purpose of the clause and the original agreement to irrevocably waive objections.
